data.title: Bristol-Myers Squibb: Your Chance To Buy A Bargain 6% Yield
data.description:
Article Thesis

Bristol-Myers Squibb (NYSE:BMY) is a high-quality company that oftentimes experiences large swings in its share price. Right now, its share price is at a low point, which results in a very low valuation and a highly attractive dividend yield of 6%. Its business is not cyclical, and the company has growth potential in different areas, which is why I am a fan of the stock at the current bargain valuation.

Past Coverage

I have written about Bristol-Myers Squibb here on Seeking Alpha in the past, most recently in June 2025, around four months ago. Since then, shares are down slightly on a net basis, although they had risen above $50 for a bit after my most recent article had been published. With more than a quarter having passed since my last update on the company, and with the company reporting its earnings results since then, while we also got some news on the politics side, it is time to update my views on Bristol-Myers Squibb.

Big Pharma And Politics

High healthcare costs are a problem for many countries, including the United States. But while some believe that high healthcare costs must be caused by high drug prices primarily, that isn't true -- according to this data, drug spending is just 9% of overall healthcare spending, with way more money going to hospitals, clinics, etc. So even if drug costs could somehow be cut in half, overall healthcare spending would decline by just a couple of percentage points. I thus believe that there are more relevant tasks if politicians want to tackle high healthcare costs instead of focusing on drug costs too much.

While the Trump administration has announced tariffs on some drug imports, that is more of a problem for non-US companies. Bristol-Myers Squibb, as a US-based company, will likely be less affected. Also, since drugs have very high gross margins, tariffs would not really affect the bottom line overly much, relative to low-margin businesses like steel, automobiles, and so on, where the tariff impact is way larger.

Even if politicians want to focus on bringing down drug prices despite them making up just a minor portion of overall healthcare spending, that does not have to be a major problem for big pharma companies like BMY. Cutting out middlemen like pharmacy benefit managers is a suitable solution, and one that doesn't hurt the producer of the drugs, like BMY. A recent deal between the Trump administration and Pfizer (PFE) was very well-received by the market, showing that efforts to bring down drug prices can actually be good for pharma investors if politicians and drug companies focus on reducing bureaucratic costs, middleman costs, and so on. I would not be surprised to see similar deals with other major pharma companies in the coming weeks, possibly including Bristol-Myers Squibb.

While a regulated industry like pharma always has some political risks, I don't think political risks are overly high when it comes to BMY: Drug prices are not actually all that impactful when it comes to healthcare spending, BMY is US-based, reducing the tariff risk, and direct-to-consumer deals with the US administration seem to be good for big pharma companies.

Bristol-Myers Squibb: Avenues For Growth

Big pharma companies oftentimes do not grow at a very fast pace, as they constantly have to battle with losses of exclusivity for some of their older drugs. New drugs that are brought to the market thus have to offset declines in other drugs, where patent protection has expired. Still, Bristol-Myers Squibb should be able to deliver some growth in the coming years, I believe.

The company's most recent quarter was substantially better than what the analyst community had expected, as Wall Street analysts were forecasting a mid-single digit revenue decline while BMY actually saw a positive revenue growth rate. This was achieved despite declines in some of BMY's major legacy drugs, such as Revlimid, which BMY got via the Celgene takeover a couple of years ago, and which experienced a sales decline of close to 40%. Pomalyst, another legacy Celgene drug, saw a sales decline of more than one-quarter as well.

The good news is that as these drugs' sales are declining in size, their future declines will have a smaller impact on a company-wide basis: If, for example, a drug drops from $500 million to $250 million, the company-wide sales hit is smaller than when it drops from $1 billion to $500 million, despite the relative decline rate being the same. BMY being able to grow its sales despite the declines in some of its legacy brands was due to nice gains in assets such as Yervoy and Opdivo, which grew by 16% and 7%, respectively, versus one year earlier, with combined sales of more than $3 billion during a single quarter. Hematology drug Breyanzi did very well, seeing sales rise by a hefty 125%, now with a run rate of well north of $1 billion a year. Camzyos was another fast-growing drug during Q2, with sales jumping 87% to just above a quarter-billion dollars during the quarter. The top-line contribution for BMY of these fast-growing drugs will rise quickly if they maintain their very positive momentum going forward.

Bristol-Myers Squibb expects many major data points in the foreseeable future, including on BMY's Alzheimer's Disease candidate and on Krazati in first-line non-small cell lung cancer, which are two very big markets, with Iza-bren in solid tumors being interesting as well, I believe. Recent deals with companies such as BioNTech (BNTX) have further strengthened BMY's pipeline, and the company has the financial firepower to engage in M&A in the future, too:

While BMY has a considerable debt position, its business is throwing off a lot of cash -- around $15 billion over the last year, with capital expenditures already being accounted for. The dividend costs around $5 billion per year, thus the company has $10 billion in surplus cash that is available for M&A, debt reduction, or share repurchases. All of these would create value in the long run:

- M&A would strengthen the pipeline and add future revenue and profit

- Debt reduction would shift value from debt holders to equity holders, assuming BMY's enterprise value remains unchanged, while also reducing interest expenses and thereby growing profits

- Share repurchases give all remaining investors a larger portion of the pie and grow cash flow per share and earnings per share

I think pursuing a mix of these is a good idea, but due to the very low valuation BMY trades at today -- its earnings yield is around 15% -- share repurchases seem highly attractive to me right now.

With tailwinds from an aging population in many major markets that requires more healthcare, which should be good for all pharma companies, BMY thus has a positive growth outlook, I believe: It owns fast-growing new drugs, its cash cows like Opdivo keep growing as well, it has a deep pipeline, keeps forging deals with companies like BNTX, and buybacks could add to its earnings per share growth on top of that.

BMY: A Nice Yield At A Low Price

Today, at just below $45 per share, BMY trades at a little less than 7x net earnings. For a company with a resilient business model and a not-great-but-solid growth outlook, that doesn't seem fair, I believe. When we look at BMY's enterprise value to EBITDA multiple, which also accounts for debt and cash on the balance sheet, we see a very low valuation as well:

In recent months, BMY's valuation has declined meaningfully, making it an even better value today. At the same time, the price decline has made BMY's dividend yield climb to 6%, which I deem very attractive, especially when combined with a payout ratio of less than 40% -- making the dividend pretty safe.

All in all, Bristol-Myers Squibb looks quite attractive here, I believe. While politics are a factor to consider, I don't think the risks are very high, and the advantages, like a great yield and a bargain valuation, easily outweigh the risks.

data.title: Silicom Struggles To Make Ends Meet But Trades For Liquidation Value
data.description:
Silicom (NASDAQ:SILC) is an Israeli developer of network hardware, traditionally selling to on-premise server OEMs, which is slowly turning into more cloud-oriented products.

The company suffered a double-whammy of a destocking cycle that started in 2022, coupled with a missed shift in the technological landscape. This led to a collapse in sales to almost 1/3 of their peak ($58 million in FY24 vs $150 million in FY22). At this scale, the company is unprofitable.

Seen from this perspective, a hardware company that has been left somewhat behind in technology and that cannot cover its expenses should not be worth much. Indeed, that is the case, with the company trading for basically a small premium over its cash holdings. This provides some degree of optionality, which, without making it suitable for long-term investing, makes it interesting for speculation. I believe the name is a Hold, but consider that it could be a speculative Buy for some aggressive readers.

Network adapter equipment

Silicom's business has been the design of modular add-ons to servers, providing additional functionalities, like encryption, acceleration, data compression, redirection, or switching.

In particular, the company specializes in selling a variety of modules, each with different configurations. This allows customers with specific needs to select a customized application.

The company sells mostly via integration with server OEMs. These OEMs cannot (or could not) get all of the customization needed from the original chip or PCB designers (Intel, Broadcom, NVIDIA), and therefore used Silicom's products to enhance the functionality of their products and serve more niche markets.

R&D with a focus on D

From the financials, product, and even name, Silicom is a tech, R&D-focused company. Almost half of its employees work in R&D, and if we remove manufacturing employees, research would be 75% of the company (20-F under Total Employees). R&D also makes up 2/3 of the company's OpEx ($20 million out of $30 million in FY24), and has not been cut even under the company's current challenging situation

However, three factors lead me to add some nuance around the technological focus of the company.

First, the company never achieved super exciting gross margins (peaking at 40% over a decade ago). High gross margins, even in hardware businesses, are a sign that the product can be sold for a premium, which embeds the 'intangible technological component'. Indeed, this is the reason why software, which is almost entirely intangible, has 80/90% gross margins sometimes.

Second, the company records several patents between 2012 and 2015, then one in 2018, and then no other (see 20-F under Intellectual Property). This fall in patenting activity is coincident with a fall in gross margins that continues up to this point.

And finally, the company seems to have lost track of the technological trend, or has been unable to steer the course of its developments, as seen in its collapsing revenues (more on this below).

This leads me to believe that at least part of that R&D expense is what can be considered 'Design'. That is, work performed specifically for a customer in order to win a design (get a component inside of the customer's product), which does not confer a specifically valuable technological advantage that can be patented or a moat built around.

This type of Design-Win dynamic is very common in all kinds of industries where a group of component designers and manufacturers work around a central OEM. One such example is vehicle manufacturing. These companies can look innovative because they have lots of engineers, but are not necessarily developing any cutting-edge products.

Design-to-win companies tend to suffer from not incredible economics and low bargaining power, given that the OEM controls distribution and has access to clients, and that it can purchase from a series of component designers. The company invests in designing a particular component before landing a design-win, and therefore has an incentive to recover those investments by winning the design, even if the margins or conditions are not the best.

A collapse in sales that cannot be blamed on inventories

The company had already shown some challenges in decreasing margins, but had not posted problems with revenues. Quite the contrary, even if growth was not fast, the company was moving upwards in revenues.

This changed after 2022, since when the company's sales have fallen to almost 1/3 of their peak, in less than 3 years.

On the MD&A of its FY24 20-F (which includes explanations for FY23), the company reports that the fall in revenues was generated mostly by a destocking cycle at its customers' level. This can be understandable, given that many industries suffered from overstocking and then destocking in the 2021/22 aftermath period.

However, it cannot cover the entirety of the process, first because of the degree of the decline in sales, and second because that kind of cycle tends to repeat over time, but we can't find any such instances of such a magnitude in the company's revenues in the past.

There are two indications that at least one additional driver is technological. First, the company recognizes on its 20-F (under Trends) that the movement form on-promise servers to cloud-based servers has made some of its product lines obsolete, while enhancing others. Second, the company announced in early 2024 a 5-year plan to recover its profitability, which included phasing off some product lines (for example, Server Adapters) and focusing on others (like Smart Platforms), which are more in line with new technological trends.

Unprofitable at its current scale

After the revenues collapsed, the company cut some expenses, with the number of employees falling from 306 in 2022 to 229 in 2024 (not reported for 2Q25 so far). The enormous majority of these cuts were in Manufacturing, which makes sense if the company is selling less.

However, the company is currently not able to cover most of its operating expenses at this scale, and has maintained a loss of around $13/15 million operationally per year, which is not improving in recent quarters.

In recent earnings calls, the company has not talked about continuing with OpEx cuts. Rather, I believe it expects to grow into profitability (more on this in the valuation section).

Financially sound

If there is one strength that Silicom has, it is its balance sheet. The company has no debt, has a positive working capital of almost $35 million, and has cash and securities of $79 million (2Q25 results, $52 million cash and equivalents, $12 million current marketable securities, and $15 million non-current marketable securities).

This allows the company to sustain the above losses for years without entering into any potential financial problems. It also allows SILC to carry out some share buybacks, specifically almost $10 million in each FY23 and FY24 (see historical cashflows).

Unfortunately, the company does not publish cash flows on a quarterly basis. However, looking at the FY24 cash flows, they were very moderately negative on an operating basis without working capital. The company posted a net loss of almost $14 million, but when we add back depreciation (~$2 million), SBC (~$3 million), and non-cash impairments (~$6 million), the cash outflow from operations is actually closer to $3 million only. In addition, the company reduced its working capital, generating a positive $18 million inflow from operations.

If we look at this year, I would expect the cash flow losses to be larger. The reason is that this year, the company has been posting similar operating losses, but in this case, they cannot be blamed on non-cash impairments. Still, considering similar D&A and SBC levels to last year, the cash losses should not be above $8 million before working capital, indicating the company has years at this level of operations before any financial trouble appears.

Not interesting for investment, potentially for speculation

What would the company need in order to become profitable, assuming that it does not cut (nor grow) its OpEx?

From OpEx of $30 million, and assuming a current gross margin of about 32%, SILC would need about $94 million in revenues to break even operationally. This is almost 60% and $36 million above its current TTM revenue levels.

On its 5-year plan, the company set an objective of $3 in EPS by 2028.

How much that level of EPS represents in net income is hard to say without knowing if the company has continued to repurchase shares, or if it will do so even more in the future. Based on the 2Q25 average weighted shares (5.7 million), it would mean about $17 million in net income, or close to $175 million in revenues, assuming no increase in OpEx or improvement in gross margins ($17 million in net income is $22 million in pre-tax income before 25% taxes, which added to $30 million in OpEx and 32% gross margins implies $175 million in revenues).

SILC is starting to show some signs of improvement, with 7 to 9 design wins guided for FY25 (about 4 already announced, each for between $1 and $3 million annual run rate at full ramp up). Based on a $2 million average win, and assuming no design losses (super optimistic assumption in such a technological market), the company could add $18 million in revenues per year at this rate, or reach break-even in 2027, and probably not meet the $3 EPS objective by 2028. That is with two optimistic expectations, like flat OpEx, and no loss of previous contracts.

However, that scenario is heavily discounted in today's stock price. Indeed, the company basically trades for its liquidation value (net working capital plus cash and securities of about $113 million versus a market cap of about $100 million).

That means the market already discounts the current scenario, where SILC generates no profitability, and eventually either liquidates or eats its cash balances slowly. The positive scenarios become optionality.

In my opinion, given the company's challenging competitive position (explained in the Design section), the failures of the past in maintaining market, and the fact that it requires significant growth to reach break-even, all lead me to believe the company will not generate profits soon, or ever, and that it is therefore not attractive for investment (as it is not planning to liquidate).

For this reason, I consider the name a Hold. However, I do believe that some readers might find the company attractive for speculative purposes, given that the most probable negative scenario is discounted, whereas the much less probable but still existing scenarios of success are not.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Air Liquide: A Hidden Backbone Of The AI Era And The Hydrogen Economy
data.description:
Air Liquide (OTCPK:AIQUF, OTCPK:AIQUY) is one of the world’s leading industrial gas and specialty chemical companies potentially positioned well to benefit from industry tailwinds for clean-energy hydrogen projections, electrification and semiconductor demand. As part of my ongoing expansion into the chemical space, I am initiating coverage for Air Liquide to assess whether its leadership in gasses and growth drivers in tech and energy transition are strong enough to offset a soft macroeconomic backdrop.

Air Liquide Specializes In Industrial Gasses With Energy Transition Growth Opportunities

The core business of Air Liquide is Gas & Services which generates 97% of the revenues, the remainder is Engineering & Technologies. The company has five business segments:

  1. Large Industries
  2. Industrial Merchant
  3. Healthcare
  4. Electronics
  5. Engineering & Technologies

The Large Industries segment provides large volumes of oxygen, nitrogen, hydrogen and other industrial gasses to heavy industry customers such as steelmakers, petrochemical plants and energy companies. These sales are typically generated through long-term contracts providing some shielding. However, the segment is not immune to the cyclical nature of the end markets. Growth drivers include growing demand for hydrogen for refining and clean fuel and decarbonization.

The Industrial Merchant sector focuses on providing smaller customers in the food and beverage, electronics and pharmaceutical industries with gasses. The company benefits from growth in pharmaceutical and electronics demand. This segment benefits from strong pricing power.

The Healthcare segment provides medical gases to hospitals, clinics and home-care services. The demand for healthcare never stops and that makes the segment counter-cyclical. We also note that with higher life expectancy, demand for home-based care is also rising and technological innovation in healthcare, often requiring medical gasses, also never stops.

Air Liquide provides high purity gasses and advanced materials for semiconductor manufacturing and photovoltaics. The use of gasses in semiconductor manufacturing is often not highlighted, but without gasses manufacturing would not be possible. Current high demand for chips provides a solid growth driver for gasses and advanced materials.

The Engineering and Technologies segment builds and sells gas production and purification plants, hydrogen and carbon dioxide solutions.

Putting it all together, I do believe that the company has significant exposure to cyclical markets and also the semiconductor end market heavily depends on investment cycles. At the same time, the healthcare market is counter-cyclical, and I believe Air Liquide could play a pivot role in clean energy projects, particularly clean hydrogen and decarbonization projects.

Air Liquide Shows Resilience In A Turbulent Market

In the first half of the year, the company reported sales growth of 2.6% to €13.7 billion and 1.8% comparable growth. EBITDA increased 5.1% to €3.8 billion indicating margin expansion from 28.6% to 29.3%. This was driven by pricing strength and initiatives to simplify the organization. Operating income increased 5.2% and 7.2% on comparable basis to €2.74 billion indicating margin expansion from 19.4% to almost 20%. Excluding the impact of fluctuations in energy prices, which are an input cost to the business, the margins would have increased 100 bps.

Large industry sales increased 1% to €1.74 billion in Q2 on solid activity in the Americas and the start up of an Air Separation Unit in China. Industrial Merchant sales grew 2% to €3.05 billion on flat gas volumes and lower hardware sales offset by solid pricing power. Electronics sales declined 2% to €600 million but still showed growth for the first half of the year. Healthcare sales rose 5% to €1.09 billion driven by pricing and volumes for Home Healthcare solutions.

Air Liquide Deserves A Buy Rating

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener which uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions, and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

Price targets reflect the assessment of the listing at the Euronext Paris.

Air Liquide is not a high-growth name, but I do believe that the company offers upside. I am assigning a buy rating with a $232.81 price target, indicating 18% upside. We do note the exposure to cyclical markets. However, the company is expected to grow EBITDA and free cash flow by 6-7 percent annually, driving the double-digit upside that I typically look for.

Conclusion: Air Liquide Has Appealing Exposure

The exposure to large industrial companies may not seem appealing. However, also cyclical markets rebound and I do believe that within the cyclical markets demand for clean energy such as green hydrogen will increase. Furthermore, we note the exposure to countercyclical market, pricing power and an appealing combination of service the semiconductor markets and opportunities for green hydrogen and decarbonization. As a result, I do believe the current stock price offers a compelling entry point for investors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Imperial Brands: Positive Long-Term Prospects
data.description:
I last checked on the British tobacco stock Imperial Brands PLC (OTCQX:IMBBY) (OTCQX:IMBBF) around a year ago, at which time. Its performance on the stock markets exceeded its peers'. Nothing has changed since. It still leads among the five biggest Big Five tobacco stocks by market capitalisation (see chart below).

In fact, its rise of over 49% in the past year actually exceeds the 42% increase clocked last year. While at that time, there were still good reasons to buy IMBBY, the question now is whether they sustain. The short answer is yes. Here, I explain why, along with the risks to IMBBY.

Performance and outlook are good

Yesterday, the company released its pre-close trading update for the last financial year (FY25, year ending September 2025). With this, the stock broke its four-day losing streak and jumped by 3.4%, the biggest single-day gain since early April. The update was essentially positive, with the company affirming that it has performed as per guidance and more. Here are the highlights:

  • Performance in line with guidance

Before anything else, Imperial Brands reported its H1 FY25 results in May. So it's good to get a confirmation that everything is on track before another month goes by, when it releases its full-year results.

The company doesn't mention any change to guidance, which also remained unchanged after the H1 FY25 results (see slide below). The projection of "low-single-digit net revenue growth" is a bit of a downer after a 4.6% increase last year. This is especially as the company expects a 12-14% increase in NGP net revenues, halving from the 26.4% increase last year. However, the fact that adjusted operating profit and EPS growth are expected to be sustained from last year is still positive.

  • 2030 strategy

The company also affirmed its focus on the long-term strategy set out in March (see chart below) saying "This is the fifth and final year of our 2021 strategy and our FY25 performance provides a strong foundation as we look ahead with confidence to the next phase of our strategy to 2030."

With regards to the specifics of the strategy, I'd like to draw attention to two aspects. One, that the company expects "double-digit" growth in net NGP revenues. In other words, growth required to pivot away from tobacco is well and truly expected to sustain over the next year. This is particularly encouraging considering the pressure on vapes in recent years (discussed more in the section on risks).

Two, "high-single digit rate" growth in EPS is a healthy increase too. Assuming that starting from FY25 and every year until FY30, the EPS grows by 7%, the number will be at GBP 4.46 by the end of the year, which is 1.5x the figure posted in FY24.

  • Boosting shareholder returns

Next, it also announced share buybacks worth GBP 1.45 billion for the current financial year, which amounts to 4.4% of the current market capitalisation. This adds to the passive returns, along with dividends, which aren't bad either.

As per Seeking Alpha, the stock's forward dividend yield is at 5.15%, which is slightly higher than the average of 5.08% for the big five tobacco stocks mentioned earlier. The highlight statement with regard to shareholder returns in the update is as follows:

Taking dividends and buyback together, we expect our capital returns to shareholders will exceed £2.7 billion in the coming fiscal year, representing around 11% of our current market capitalisation. "

Still attractive market multiples

The update is more positive than not, but with the robust price rise over the past year, are the stock's market multiples still attractive? Turns out, they are.

The key multiple to focus on now is for FY26. With a 7% EPS growth rate this results in an adjusted EPS of GBP 3.40 (USD 4.42) and a forward P/E of 9.44x. Incidentally, this forward P/E is lower than the 9.54x level it was at the last time I checked. It's also significantly lower than its peers' average of 14.67x, implying over 50% upside to the stock.

However, IMBBY's 5-year average forward P/E is at a smaller 8.49x, suggesting that from its own perspective, IMBBY is now richly priced. This needs to be considered along with other risks to the stock (as in the next section) for short-term investors.

At the same time, with the company having provided a vision for up to 2030 now, a long-term forecast indicates that IMBBY continues to look attractive. Based on the estimates above, EPS would land at GBP 4.46 (USD 5.79) by the year. This results in a forward P/E of 7.2x, which is more attractive compared to the 5-year average.

Risks to consider

Despite some signs of stretched valuations, in a year of macroeconomic uncertainty, it's little wonder that stocks offering assured income are trading at a premium. This is likely to continue in the next 6-12 months but at some point, there will be a tilt away from safer to riskier stocks. So if not anytime this year, then it's possible that IMBBY will see some correction next year.

Next is the continued pressure on vaping. After receiving marketing denials for its products in the US in the past, the UK recently banned disposable vapes due to environmental concerns as well as the risk of addiction to nicotine among young adults through vapes. More measures are underway, like a vaping tax and a ban on vaping and a ban on advertisements. These developments can continue to slow down the company's progress away from pure tobacco products.

What's next?

Despite the risks though, there are overwhelming positives for Imperial Brands right now, even if the price increases were to slow down. The company's long-term outlook is encouraging, indicating the potential for capital gains. Additionally, its passive returns continue to look rather good too.

There is one risk to it, which is the still uncertain future of NGPs, as evident from tighter policies around vapes. This can slow down their growth at a time when the tobacco market is expected to only contract. For now, though, the company is moving along just fine, even with these challenges. I'm retaining a Buy rating on IMBBY.

data.title: AppLovin: The Leanest Adtech Machine Ever Built
data.description:
AppLovin Corporation (NASDAQ:APP) stock has surged 86% from our first analysis, marking an exceptional bull run in just one quarter. AppLovin's Q3 2025 earnings are expected to be announced November 5 as it is put through one of the leanest of all of the digital ads business models. Previously an online gaming monetization platform, AppLovin rebranded as an AI-infused advertising infrastructure giant that is best-in-class for profitability, automation, and precision.

Riding on its all-time second quarter, hopes now hang on whether its in-house self-service AXON Ads Manager can take the adoption beyond gaming into e-commerce and non-gaming brands internationally. As consensus estimates indicate another strong quarter of development and solidity, AppLovin's case remains clear as day with exact execution, compounding efficiency, and-scalable intelligence as its next gear of development drivers.

AppLovin Q3 of 2025 Earnings Preview

AppLovin is about to release third-quarter results for the year 2025 on November 5, as it hopes for another quarterly milestone. In Q2 of 2025, it achieved $1.26 billion revenue in year-on-year surged by 77%, $772 million net income in year-on-year surged by 156%, and $1.02 billion adjusted EBITDA with industry-leading 81% margin. It also realized $768 million in free cash flow, re-establishing AppLovin as one of the most profitable in the industry of adtech.

For Q3, management is guiding $1.32–$1.34 billion in revenue and $1.07–$1.09 billion in adjusted EBITDA, mid-single-digit sequential growth for another quarter with margins in the range of 81%. EPS estimates come in at $2.58 on $1.34 billion in sales, higher than Q2's $2.49 and $1.26 billion. Thirteen analysts, in the last 90 days, raised estimates, a rare unanimous thumbs-up vote.

Investors also need to watch for whether net revenue per install, up by 70% YoY in the prior quarter, continues to bounce back, an indicator that AppLovin's AI-ad engine is gearing up faster than the peers. At $9.15 in FY25 EPS as well as $13.80 in FY26 EPS, analysts project 51% EPS growth next year and a PEG ratio near 0.4x, suggesting the valuation reflects compounding earnings rather than speculative growth.

AppLovin also trades around a 36x forward P/S, well out of its long-term mean of 11x, in software-like efficiency of adtech cyclicality. On an estimated 2026 revenue of $7.4 billion, the multiple drops to roughly 28x, implying valuation expansion is being canceled out by fast-fundamental growth. If AXON Ads Manager replicates globally as planned, that premium is a vote of faith in lasting, algorithm-driven compounding, not speculative excess.

Gaming Arbitrage to Advertising Infrastructure

AppLovin's transformation is a classic case of strategic redefinition. The company used to nearly solely rely on the owned gaming inventory for creating advertising inventory. That reinvention was necessary to break through a ceiling defined by limited reach and the cyclicality of gaming performance. Under CEO Adam Foroughi’s leadership, AppLovin dismantled its old business model, completing the sale of its Apps segment to Tripledot Studios in June 2025 for $400 million in cash plus a 20% ownership stake. That was more than a divestment, it marked the end of an era for AppLovin.

By abandoning owned gaming, AppLovin repositioned as a pure advertising infrastructure platform. Its MAX mediation system controls proprietary mobile in-app supply, and its machine learning engine, AXON, powers real-time placement and optimization of ads. Together, they've turned AppLovin into an AI-native marketplace for ads, where algorithms, rather than human sales forces, determine where the dollars flow.

The New Frontier: The Self-Serve Revolution and AXON Ads Manager

If AppLovin's past two years were consolidation and streamlining, this next phase is all about scale, driven by AXON Ads Manager, an in-house platform for ads that is specifically designed for bringing online all manner of businesses from huge global brands to small Shopify stores. It was quietly rolled out in Q2, with credit card billing, automation of onboarding, and dynamic creative building tools, with further mass availability in store from October 1, 2025 by referral. It is awaiting to launch globally in the first half of the year 2026.

The stakes are huge. Historically, AppLovin's revenue was generated primarily from gaming advertisements. Now, through AXON Ads Manager, the firm is increasing its addressable reach into e-commerce and larger direct-to-consumer brands, an industry that is far larger than the world of mobile gaming. The CEO stated that they’ve got one of the most profitable financial models the world has ever seen.

The roll architecture is strategized. Referral-driven Q4 onboarding enables AppLovin scale gradual while optimizing user experience as well as conversion flows. Early signs suggest strong hunger with hundreds of turnarounds of e-commerce advertisers already in turn have piloted the platform, creating what management called extremely incremental revenue gain.

The scaling isn’t top-line growth, but data. It is data each new campaign and advertiser contributes to the training set of the AXON, such that predictive models refine and conversion targeting becomes more effective. As the data set extends from gaming into shopping and lifestyle verticals, the cross-domain knowledge of consumer behavior of the AXON deepens further, a competitive edge that will expand AppLovin's moat in the long term.

Risk Lens: High Stakes Maintain a Thin Margin of Error

Even the best narratives have pressure points. AppLovin's valuation is based on seamless execution with not much cushion for miss. There are three key risks that become evident. First, valuation compression risk. Approached 60x forward earnings and 50x forward EV/EBITDA, the stock multiple assumes double-digit consistent growth and flawless retention. Anything that decelerates, such as macro softness in ads, lower CPMs, or slower online spend adoption, would lead to a multiple resetting, but fundamentals being okay.

Second, competitors and regulation headwinds. It is still in the walled gardens of the ads of mobiles, where much of the data and the distribution of an ecosystem is controlled by Google and Meta. While AppLovin's AI-first platform gives it access to cost leadership, going global means bringing with it new needs for data governance and compliance that could slow the rollouts' pace.

Third, risk of execution and focus. While the customer base of the firm is big, the firm's demand is still based in gaming, an industry that pendulums with global app assessment trends. Shifting to non-gaming advertisers must occur quickly in order to sustain the current pace of growth.

Still, even when accounting for these risks, AppLovin’s underlying financial resilience provides a strong cushion. The balance sheet remains light, cash flow is substantial, and debt maturities are well managed. The company has the flexibility to experiment, fail fast, and iterate, luxuries that most adtech competitors cannot afford.

Bottom Line

AppLovin's resurgence is more than a case of momentum; it is proof of the company's evolution into an AI-driven advertising infrastructure platform. As it continues in expansion beyond gaming with the self-serve deployment, AppLovin's efficiency, automation, and data-driven accuracy create a lasting foundation for compounding growth. As much as hype was hyped, its consistency of execution and widening ecosystem put it among the few adtech operators converting scale and smarts into sustained profitability.

data.title: Pitanium: Struggle To Justify Its Valuation
data.description:
Pitanium (NASDAQ:PTNM) went public around four months ago, and what’s happening now feels like the classic post-IPO hype.

The fundamentals don’t really line up with the rally, which has already more than doubled the company’s market cap.

You've heard this story before, right?

Gamehaus (GMHS), MasterBeef (MB), and plenty of other overseas holding companies running operations through Chinese subsidiaries go public in the U.S., and then the same cycle kicks in.

Looking at Pitanium specifically, the company has already been operating in Hong Kong for at least twelve years from the subsidiary 'Here We Seoul Limited' with its main premium beauty brands 'PITANIUM' and 'BIG PI.' Even back then, it was already operating with an asset-light model, meaning it didn’t manufacture its own products.

Instead, it leaned on both local and international OEM and ODM suppliers, kind of like Softto (SFTO), another Chinese brand that’s still waiting to wrap up its own IPO.

Possibly already aiming for an IPO, the company went through some internal reshuffling, setting up Pitanium and then acquiring Here We Seoul, which it now controls as a holding company based in the British Virgin Islands.

Was the Stock Cheap at the IPO?

Pitanium offered 1.75 million Class A shares priced at $4 per share. This essentially increased the number of shares to 22.75 million (an increase of ~8.3%), with a very small float.

The initial target was to raise ~$7.9 million, but the company only raised $7 million before expenses. I don't have information on whether the over-allotment of 262,500 shares was exercised, but either way, it wouldn't make much of a difference going forward.

Doing the math, the company was valued at exactly $91 million—right in the microcap range.

Since reported revenue for the entire FY 2024 was $9.6 million, Pitanium went public with an EV/S ratio of an incredible ~9.5x! This is 578% higher than the median multiple of Consumer Staples and most of its peers within the Luxury Cosmetics category:

  • Estée Lauder (EL): 2.6x
  • Coty (COTY): 1.3x
  • Interparfums (IPAR): 2.2x
  • Softto (my projections based on the IPO prospectus): 8.4x
  • Waldencast (WALD): 1.5x

You might think Pitanium is receiving this huge premium over its peers because of its extremely strong sales, right? Not really. Sales were up only 10.6% year-over-year in FY 2024 and have basically stalled at 0.1% growth over TTM.

A few other things to keep in mind that back up my point that this stock wasn't exactly a bargain even at $4:

  1. Even though the company maintains a strong gross profit (typically ~80% of total revenue, typical for asset-light companies in this sector), almost all of it is spent on SG&A expenses to leverage the brand. This has generally led to very small and declining EBIT margins (17.9% in FY 2023, 14.6% in FY 2024, and 7.7% in the TTM), as well as a very small net income in all observed years (EPS of $0.05 in FY 2024, essentially an 80x P/E at $4).
  2. The company’s sitting on just $1.5 million in cash, which is a very thin liquidity cushion, barely covering its short-term debt of $1.4 million. There's also a small build-up in inventory, which could point to some inefficiencies in managing operating assets and a need for more working capital.
  3. Even with its asset-light setup, which should keep Capex really low (Pitanium spends no more than $500,000 a year), the company struggles to generate cash. Over the trailing twelve months, operating cash flow came in at -$1.1 million, barely enough to cover Capex of $300,000. At the end of the day, they are burning cash. In my opinion, if another negative OCF occurs, they would most likely need to take on even more debt or begin selling their assets (six retail stores in high-end shopping destinations in Hong Kong and three warehouses).

Post-IPO Trends: What Now?

After the initial surge and the plateau that followed, the stock took off again, probably not surprising given the extremely low float.

Now trading above $10, it's up to us to take a step back, look at the fundamentals, and see whether this rally actually has any real support.

Looking at the macro side, we first need to ask whether there's real demand for premium beauty in Hong Kong.

When you check out what Pitanium is selling, their products easily outperform the affordable segment—their serums at $80–130 pull in at least four times the sales of something like CeraVe, and their Face Waters beat mid-range brands like Paula’s Choice by three to four times.

So, we need to look at very specific trends.

What we know:

  • There has been significant growth in visitors to Hong Kong, coming very close to pre-pandemic numbers. In July, for example, there were ~4.39 million tourists (a 12.5% ​​increase year-over-year), but still 800,000 fewer than in 2019. That said, the government is still pushing domestic tourism, and it looks like it's paying off in Macau. I'd expect Hong Kong tourism to return to normal in two or three years, assuming we don't hit a global recession.

  • Hong Kong’s GDP grew 3.1% year-over-year in Q2 2025, ticking up 10 basis points from the previous quarter. It’s still about 200 basis points behind China, whose growth has been slowing.

  • Even with a recovery in 2024–2025, the HK50 hasn't reached the highs of 2018 or 2021. Add in a weak real estate market—residential prices have been dropping since 2022, with the house price index near five-year lows—and you get a pretty muted wealth effect. In short, lower property values ​​could make wealthy Hong Kong residents pull back on luxury spending. And this is very interesting, since real estate and the stock market are the two largest pillars of wealth there.

  • Perhaps driven by these two factors I just mentioned, Business Confidence reached -10 points in Q1 2025, the worst reading in four years, when Hong Kong was still recovering from the pandemic. Interestingly, there was a slight improvement to -9 points in Q2 and -8 points in Q3, but overall, the outlook is still pretty grim.

  • Youth unemployment hit 7.8% in August, even higher than during the inflation spike in 2022. At the same time, company vacancy rates are low, which makes me think businesses are hiring fewer people (though the numbers might look a bit different if you focus on the tourism sector).

All of this leads me to believe that the outlook for luxury goods spending is weak in Hong Kong. On the macro level, we won't see any tailwinds for a strong sales increase in FY 2025.

Is Stock Still Expensive?

Before jumping into valuation, I want to run a quick sensitivity analysis using the two most measurable ways Pitanium could deploy its IPO proceeds and what kind of incremental revenue they might generate.

They are:

  1. Develop a mobile app to improve customer experience and have a 1P sales channel (15% of funds raised, approximately $1.05 million if we consider ~$7 million after expenses); and
  2. Develop a new home care line to complement its multi-brand strategy (20% of the money raised, approximately $1.4 million after IPO expenses).

Let’s map out three specific scenarios using an ROI-based approach:

  • Bear: ~2% revenue increase in Year 1 (likely launched in FY 2026) + new pipeline delivering 0.8x, around $1.12 million. This would give us total revenue of ~$11 million (+13.4% TTM).
  • Base: ~6% revenue increase in Year 1 (likely launching in FY 2026) + new line delivering 2x, around $2.8 million. This would give us total revenue of ~$13 million (+35.4% TTM).
  • Bull: ~12% revenue increase in Year 1 (likely launching in FY 2026) + new line delivering 4x, around $5.6 million. This would give us total revenue of ~$16.3 million (+70% TTM).

That said, based on revenue projections, we arrive at the following EV/S multiples for Pitanium:

Just to dig a little deeper, if Pitanium maintains the EBITDA margin at ~10—11%, my scenarios would deliver an EBITDA between $1.1—1.43 million. These are EV/EBITDA multiples ranging from 167.5—215.9x.

Extremely stretched.

My rating on this one is a 'Strong Sell' at current levels. Pitanium’s still caught up in the post-IPO hype, but over time, the stock will have to come back to its fundamentals. I’d put fair value somewhere around 1–1.5x sales, which leaves plenty of room for downside.

data.title: I Regret Missing Out On Dell, But It's Not Too Late
data.description:
Introduction

Dell Technologies Inc. (NYSE:DELL) became one of the most interesting AI infrastructure stocks. I wanted to break down Dell after I sold my stake in Super Micro Computer (SMCI), which was about one year ago, but somehow I never did that. I cover a lot of different names, and let’s agree that Dell isn’t the sexiest in the AI sector. This company has been viewed as a mature PC manufacturer for years, with steady results, which, maybe, even made it boring for the market. I am not an exception.

The stock surged more than 9% after the company’s October 7th Analyst Day. The rally was driven by the realization of the analysts and the market that Dell is now positioned at the heart of the AI infrastructure, which will only grow in the next decade.

Now that the stock is trading close to its all-time highs, investors are naturally asking what to do next (to be honest, mostly I am asking myself what I should do). Is this a stock to chase at the top, or is Dell a long-term story that still hasn’t fully played out?

In my view, short term, Dell may have run ahead of itself, even though the valuation is still cheap. But long term, this could be one of those companies that people will regret overlooking a few years from now.

Financial Overview

I’d like to start this article from the recent results, just to get a sense of what has been happening and what revenues and growth we are talking about. Dell got $29.8 billion in revenue in the recent quarter. It was up 19% compared to the same quarter last year. Operating income was at $1.8 billion, which gives Dell a growth of 27% year-over-year. Looks impressive, of course, but if you divide that operating income by total revenue, you would see what the market is worried about. First of all, their margins aren’t very high (which is totally normal, I’ll talk more about it later), but also, operating income, while growing, is still stuck at not-so-high levels.

At the same time, while breaking Dell down, it becomes clear that this is a company in transition. Their revenues come from two segments: Infrastructure Solutions Group (ISG) and Client Solutions Group (CSG). ISG is its AI and data center business. Only recently has it become their growth driver and the bigger revenue stream. Of course, we expect the breakdown between segments to shift further in the AI direction as time goes on. They sold AI infrastructure for about $10 billion in the first half of this year, which is already more than in the last year. Management says they expect shipments to reach $20 billion for the full fiscal year.

The other half of the business, CSG, includes PCs and business computers. Basically, it’s what we know Dell for. This part of the business is essentially flat, with revenue of $12.5 billion, up just 1% year-over-year. This segment is already mature and unlikely to grow meaningfully over the next decade. But that’s okay, and even expected, because we want to see the growth in ISG. This is where the money and the attention are. And again, for the first time, this infrastructure segment has become larger than the traditional PC business, which is good news.

Dell expects total fiscal 2026 revenue to reach $107 billion, up 12% year-over-year, and earnings per share to grow about 25% to around $8. That’s a remarkable acceleration for a company that was dismissed for a long time for being cyclical and low-growth.

2025 Dell Technologies Securities Analyst Meeting

Michael Dell, the CEO of the company, said at this event that hardware is cool again, and he is very right. The message is simple but on point:

Hardware is cool again, and we are uniquely positioned, providing opportunities to grow across both data center infrastructure and AI -- and AI PCs.

Everyone is excited, of course, about AI software, but the entire ecosystem depends on the hardware on which the software runs. Over the past two years (since the last conference like this one), Dell has completely restructured its infrastructure business around AI. The company is now a major supplier of high-performance computing clusters, working directly with Nvidia (NVDA), Advanced Micro Devices (AMD), and Intel (INTC). COO Jeff Clarke explained that Dell was the first to market with the GB200 and GB300 rack-scale systems, deploying over 110,000 GPUs in a single data center in just a few weeks. After Dell ships these AI systems to customers, they’re fully operational in the range of 24 to 36 hours, and no competitor can match the results like this.

First to market with the GB200, 2 to 6 months ahead of our competition. First to market earlier this year with the GB300. We put 110,000 GPUs, a liquid cool infrastructure in a data center that was operational in weeks. That's 27,500 nodes, 1,536 racks and over 6,000 switches. All up and very short or a handful of 6 weeks, driving tens of trillions of tokens when it's up and operational and has laid the foundation for this, which is the best in the industry.

When we deliver a GB200 rack or a GB300 rack to our customer, it's up and operational in the data center and up in 24 to 36 hours. From our dock to our customer, installed in their side 24 to 36 hours. We believe it is a huge differentiator, and it's why we're winning in the marketplace today.

It’s very important because it gives Dell a huge competitive advantage - speed. And while we are not usually looking at the speed when talking of AI (software works instantly), here, in hardware, it’s very important. And you could understand why by a simple example: imagine you’re looking to buy a new fridge or dishwasher. One shop can bring it to you in three days, but the fridge is more expensive. Another offers a better price, though you’ll be waiting a couple of weeks for delivery. Most people will easily pay a premium to get it online as fast as possible. Companies are no different, and a competitive advantage like that can also increase pricing power.

The company’s AI Factory model is now helping enterprises build and deploy AI workloads. Dell has already partnered with over 3,000 enterprise customers, which shows how quickly enterprises are moving to bring AI on-premises. Moreover, the more Dell captures customers today, the bigger interest it will have from others tomorrow, meaning establishing itself fast on the market and taking a market share from the competitors. According to management, 85% of companies plan to move generative AI workloads on-prem within the next 24 months, and it looks like Dell will benefit from most of those.

85% of enterprises plan to move GenAI on-prem within the next 24 months. And we're engaged from the very start of the journey. Before infrastructure, the heavy lifting is organizing all of their data and choosing the right models for each use case. And customers want to learn from Dell's own modernization and how we apply AI for competitive advantage in our business. And when they're ready to scale with an AI factory, Dell is already in that conversation, with a broad portfolio spanning data center to edge across all industries and company sizes. And we've already engaged with over 3,000 enterprise customers, and that's just a start.

How Dell Is Changing

During the conference, COO Jeff Clarke and ISG President Arthur Lewis both emphasized that Dell is no longer a “PC-first” company. It’s becoming an engineering-led, infrastructure-first powerhouse. Engineering is busy working on custom, cooled, high-density systems that will be built uniquely for each client, which I take as another competitive advantage. Together with Dell’s ability to deploy these massive systems at record speed, I see no reason why someone wouldn’t pick Dell.

The engineering curve is getting harder. So as much as I think engineering differentiates us, I think it continues to differentiate us because the design requirements are becoming more steep. If I put this in the context of the demands for the Dell AI Factory design are evolving at breakneck speeds because the technology coming at us is driving us to do that, whether that's power, density, cooling, it's now front and center, the architectural choices, the architectural innovation is not slowing. In fact, it's accelerating.

It’s also critical to note that Dell’s storage business is becoming increasingly important. Dell is the market leader here, #1 in compute and #1 in storage, with share positions higher than its next two competitors combined. Its PowerStore, PowerFlex, and PowerScale platforms give enterprises the ability to handle enormous amounts of random data, which is what 80% of AI workloads rely on.

PowerStore is the gold standard for enterprise storage. Named #1 in innovation and ease of use, PowerStore is now trusted by over 17,000 customers globally and has grown double digits in revenue in each of the last 5 quarters. PowerStore also comes with access to the Dell Automation Platform, which greatly simplifies infrastructure automation -- infrastructure simplicity, allowing customers to deploy their cloud operating system of choice across a wide variety of PowerEdge servers and PowerStore, ensuring agility, control and tremendous scale by being able to expand compute and storage independently.

Next, PowerFlex Ultra, our latest software-defined storage release, greatly improves storage efficiency and reliability delivering 10x9s of data availability and up to 80% storage efficiency, PowerFlex Ultra helps customers to reduce cost by maximizing resource utilization while providing robust redundancy without unnecessary replication.

Next, PowerProtect Data Domain All-Flash delivers world-class cyber resilience with speed and efficiency. With up to 544 terabytes of usable storage per node, it delivers 4x faster restore times, 2x faster replication, 80% less power consumption and 40% less floor space.

I think this is not fully priced in yet. Otherwise, the stock would go through a serious re-rating. It means that Dell is not competing only in hardware. It’s competing on architecture, and the management stressed it many times during the presentation.

Profitability and Valuation

Profitability is one of the weaker sides of Dell, or at least, this is what worries the investors. Dell’s margins aren’t impressive when compared to the sector median. The company’s gross profit margin is just over 21%, which is much lower than the tech sector average of roughly 50%.

But I would argue that it's not fair to look at Dell's profitability this way, as Dell is being compared to the information technology sector. In this sector, together with Dell, you will find a lot of different names that have a completely different business model. And as a result, they get higher gross margins. A great example is Okta (OKTA) stock, which is located in the same sector. I just broke it down last week. It's a cybersecurity company, so their gross profit margin is at 77%, which is a lot, but at the same time, it’s expected from software companies. If we were to take a look at peers of Dell for profitability, like, for example, Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE), we would see that their gross profit margin is at about 30%. It's a little higher than Dell, but at the same time, it’s not 50% of the sector median. SMCI has a gross profit margin of about 11%.

The more relevant story is valuation. Dell’s forward earnings valuation multiple is at about 16x, which is 40% lower than the sector median. Its forward PEG ratio (price to earnings divided by growth) is also about 40% below the sector. This means that, despite the recent rally, Dell is still undervalued relative to its projected growth and earnings.

The company has returned nearly 97% of its adjusted free cash flow since launching its capital return program, including dividends and buybacks. Shares outstanding dropped from 731 million to around 672 million in just the past year and a half, another major green flag.

Conclusion

Dell is still in the middle of a transformation, but at the same time, it has room to develop and expand.

In the short term, Dell stock might not be the top pick. It’s up about 8% after the conference, and in general, the stock trades not far from an all-time high. It’s also about 40% higher in valuation metrics than its own five-year average. Besides, Dell sometimes misses on earnings. Right now, analysts have nine upward and nine downward estimate revisions ahead of Q3 results, which means the stock could move either way at the end of next month.

At the same time, short-term pullbacks could create better entry points. For long-term investors, Dell remains a clear opportunity to build a position gradually at a cheap price. The company’s forward P/E and PEG both suggest that long-term it has a huge space for increase, and also its EPS growth and buyback program make the long-term play much more convenient.

data.title: EM Bonds Shine As DM Bonds Struggle With Fiscal Drift
data.description:
EM bonds continue to outperform on fiscal discipline and CNY strength, while indebted DMs struggle with weak policy and unpopular governments.

The VanEck Emerging Markets Bond ETF† was up 1.70% in September, compared to 1.59% for its benchmark, the 50% J.P. Morgan Government Bond Index - Emerging Markets Global Diversified (GBI-EM) and 50% J.P. Morgan Emerging Markets Bond Index (EMBI). Year to date, the ETF is up 15.41%, compared to 13.04% for its benchmark, and 8.12% and 6.96% for the Global Agg and 10-year Treasuries, respectively. Argentina was a big mover this month (and an outperformer for the ETF). We touch on it inside, though we caution that there is a significant global oversupply of opinions on Argentina. Here’s ours: we didn’t like Argentina because policy was way ahead of politics and there was a consensus overweight/bullish view in markets and among cab drivers globally. Then politics arose, bonds puked, and there was a chance of a fairly fast and easy bridge loan from the US (which then happened). So, when it crashed, we got bullish. It’s a trade, we are still in a heavily game-theoretic stage. We remain very bullish on local currency, while somewhat cautious on USD duration especially with low spreads. The ETF has around 55.3% in curated local currency, the rest in mostly higher-yielding USD bonds. Carry is 6.4%, yield to worst (YTW) is 8.1%, and duration is 5.3. The maximum for local is 60% and our bias remains to be close to the maximum.

Average Annual Total Returns* (%) (In USD)

As of September 30, 2025
1 Mo 3 Mo YTD 1 Yr 3 Yrs 5 Yrs 10 Yrs
EMBX (NAV) 1.70 4.40 15.41 9.72 13.39 4.87 5.27
EMBX (Share Price) 1.70 4.40 15.41 9.72 13.39 4.87 5.27
50% GBI-EM/50% EMBI 1.59 3.78 13.05 7.98 11.82 2.34 3.93

* Returns less than one year are not annualized.

Expenses: Class A: Gross 1.83%, Net 1.21%; Class I: Gross 1.37%, Net 0.86%; Class Y: Gross 1.33%, Net 0.96%. Expenses are capped contractually until 5/01/26 at 1.20% for Class A, 0.85% for Class I, 0.95% for Class Y. Caps exclude acquired fund fees and expenses, interest, trading, dividends, and interest payments of securities sold short, taxes, and extraordinary expenses.

The performance data quoted represents past performance. Past performance is not a guarantee of future results. Investment return and principal value of an investment will fluctuate so that an investor’s shares, when redeemed, may be worth more or less than their original cost.

Performance may be lower or higher than performance data quoted. Please call 800.826.2333 or visit vaneck.com for performance current to the most recent month ended.

The “Net Asset Value” (NAV) of a Fund is determined at the close of each business day , and represents the dollar value of one share of the fund; it is calculated by taking the total asset of the fund, subtracting total liabilities, and dividing by the total number of shares outstanding. The NAV is not necessarily the same of the ETF’s intraday trading value. Investors should not expect to buy or sell shares at NAV.

Prior to 10/06/2025, the Fund operated as the VanEck Emerging Markets Bond mutual fund; performance shown before that date is that fund’s NAV performance (Class I, unadjusted for today’s ETF expenses).

The big wheel turning remains “fiscal dominance,” in which low-indebted EMs with inflation-focused central banks win, and high-indebted DMs with arguably co-opted central banks lose. We got reminders of the bad – UK financing worries, a EU that looks not to be seizing the moment to issue mutualized debt when the market wants it, a new PM in France with immediate protests over fiscal policy…sigh. EM gave us more reminders of the good. Mexico’s first budget under newly elected President Sheinbaum met market expectations of orthodoxy. Indonesia re-committed to its fiscal targets after a bout of political turmoil that saw its finance minister leave. (Now, on Indonesia, we must say that we have low confidence in this commitment, but our point is that the government rushed to reassure on orthodoxy; whether they are serious is a separate question.) South Africa is arguably in a goldilocks situation, with its always-excellent monetary policy now complemented by improved fiscal outcomes. China is chugging along smoothly, with the market hopefully realizing that weak growth outcomes will be met with fiscal stimulus (which will be reduced if strong growth outcomes materialize). And we have deepening reform in Ecuador under its IMF program, and surprising positive policy tilts in Zambia, just to mention some of the off-the-beaten-path names.

“Fiscal dominance” also happens to map to social and political risk. A classic outcome is higher inflation, which reduces the real value of debt at the expense of social and political peace, as well as attempts to downplay inflation in nominal GDP narratives. A cursory look at DM headlines tells you we are at this stage. EM politics, on the other hand, are generally characterized by strength, popularity, and economic orthodoxy. Just look at Exhibit 1, which shows government popularity (defined as “approval” and using data sources chosen by Perplexity). We always like to remind investors that it is hard (and getting harder) to find relevant political parties in EMs that can get away with heterodoxy – voters aren’t as susceptible to promises of fiscal profligacy or harnessing central banks to gin up growth. Mexico’s supposedly left-wing government is economically orthodox and generated higher real incomes for Mexicans, for example – that is a typical and arguably growing formula inside EMs. Roughly half of consumption in many of our countries is food and energy, so voters have disciplined politicians over decades.

Exhibit 1 – EMs Have Popular (and Orthodox) Governments, DMs Have Unpopular and Flailing Governments

Government Popularity

If “fiscal dominance” is the big wheel, CNY remains the driving wheel where actual asset-price stuff starts happening…and it keeps strengthening steadily. This remains one of the most important dynamics for all markets and remains incredibly overlooked. EMs trade more with China than with the US, so this is a huge tailwind to EM currencies (see Exhibit 2). And remember the start of the year or even last month? The consensus view was that tariffs would destroy EM currencies. The opposite has happened. We’ve written extensively on this, including our piece “The Curiously Unpopular Case for RMB/CNY Appreciation”. Now, as we said in the piece, CNY itself is not the way to express this view. It will be tightly managed, and the appreciation will be like watching paint dry. Lucky for us, we can invest in bonds/currencies that are allowed to move more freely; see below for the big names. We should also point out that the ETF has no exposure to India local currency, which performed poorly in 2025, as we see it as broadly “not ready for primetime”. We are also cautious/underweight on Indonesia local, also a loser this year.

Exhibit 2 – EM Currencies Keep Rallying; Every Possible Cause Cited Other Than CNY

A look at Argentina. The ratio of the number of opinions on Argentina relative to its importance is astronomical. For decades this has been the case. From cab drivers to heads of state – I’m always getting asked about trivial Argentina and never about Indonesia, Brazil, South Africa, or Nigeria. It’s incredible. Our opinion is actually pretty simple, and we’re also intimating that the world doesn’t need more opinions on Argentina…but here’s ours.

We didn’t like Argentina. Policy was way ahead of politics. Then it blew up in September and bond prices crashed. And, there was a chance of a fairly fast and easy bridge loan from the US. And, the market was consensus overweight, even the cab drivers. So, when it crashed, we got bullish. It’s a trade; we are still in a heavily game-theoretic stage. But, the government can probably get through to midterm elections with protecting reserves and actually buying back short-dated USD debt (which we now own). We’ll see what we think at that next decision node, the midterm elections.

Exposure Types and Significant Changes

The changes to our top positions are summarized below. Our largest positions in September were South Africa, Mexico, Poland, Thailand and Malaysia:

  • We increased our local currency exposure in South Africa and Poland. South Africa’s “sleeper” fiscal story is not fully appreciated by the market, but the country’s fiscal performance continues to improve, and the latest move – considering a formal fiscal rule – would be a major structural breakthrough. In terms of our investment process, this has strengthened South Africa’s policy test score. Poland’s fiscal situation looks more concerning, but this is unlikely to affect net bond issuance and slow fiscal consolidation (which in part reflects less inflationary defense spending) is mostly priced in. Poland’s central bank remains credible, and the currency is a proxy for the Europe’s revival theme, which improves the technical test score for the country.
  • We also increased our hard currency sovereign exposure in Argentina and the Republic of Congo. The level of political noise in Argentina will remain elevated in the run up to the mid-term elections, but the U.S. decision to provide a large-scale support package (which came after a major selloff) is likely to be a game-changer for the post-election period, strengthening the policy test score for the country. Congo’s multi-billion dollar deal with China to boost oil production had a positive impact on the country’s economic and policy test scores.
  • Finally, we increased our local currency exposure in Mexico and Chile. Mexico is a major winner in Trade War 2.0, with supportive domestic backdrop (gradual fiscal consolidation, disinflation, and credible rate cuts being the most relevant for local bonds). The global commodity/copper price backdrop remains beneficial for the Chilean currency, the central bank might deliver another rate cut, and the presidential election outcome is not expected to derail Chile’s orthodox policy framework. In terms of our investment process, this improves the technical and policy test scores for the country.
  • We reduced our local currency exposure in Indonesia on the back of the constant flow of negative fiscal news, which reflect the government’s increasingly pro-growth policy stance. An additional structural complication is an attempt to expand the central bank’s mandate. Both factors worsened the country’s policy test score. Further, Indonesia’s economy will be negatively affected by the Grasberg mine incident – it lowered the economic test score for the country.
  • We also reduced our local currency exposure and hard currency corporate exposure in Brazil. The latter reflected corporate fraud allegations. Regarding local bonds, we took partial profits after the huge year-to-date rally, which dented the technical test score for the country. We are likely to take it back up.
  • Finally, we reduced our local currency exposure in China and hard currency sovereign exposure in Bosnia and Herzegovina. Bosnia and Herzegovina is making structural progress to access the EU funds (Growth Plan for the Western Balkans), but valuations are less attractive now, so we decided to employ the funds in more compelling opportunities. We also took partial profits in China, since the anti-involution policy push is expected to lead to higher domestic prices, and hence, higher local yields, worsening the policy test score for the country.
